item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This immediately started to piss me off when I saw that it required belief in the entire earth being already evacuated.

Then I read further, and I love it. The concept, the false flag/covert attempts to preserve violence and phase it out so as not to provoke further violence, and the faction within the Foundation working against their newly ethical peers. Suspension of disbelief required a bit to imagine a canonical Foundation working with heightened empathy in the future, as their utilitarian code is basically how they preserve containment, but that's dealt with in the post I guess.

Fantastic SCP! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Read it all.
-1
You had my upvote until the end, where you were like, but we have to kill people, cause grimdark best!

In an altruistic future, there would be no need for D-Class, you would have volunteers for the dangerous work. You say so yourself:

A violent interaction is one where a human causes or attempts to cause deliberate physical harm to a non-consenting human with the desired outcome being injury or death

non-consenting being the key word.
While nobody is going to willingly be tortured for the hell of it, an civilization of altruists will definitely have brave or adventurous volunteers who will willingly endanger themselves for the sake of the group.

This pretty much just asserts that SCP scientists are nothing more than sadists.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very late, but I certainly appreciate the alternative perspective. It does not change my vote but does greatly improve my opinion of you (not that I had a bad opinion at all — I simply respect how much thought you have put into this). This was an interesting read and it reinforces, for me, that this is a clever article deserving of a read, and that you've done as good a job with it as you could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not a fan of these SCPs that take place in the future, post-Earth. I know there's no canon and all, but it really breaks immersion for me. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is it Keter? It's found only on Earth, which has only six humans on it, and nowhere else. It's perfectly contained. It should be Euclid instead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Officer please! I can't be held responsible for throwing a molotov at that post office, it was a pathogen in the air specific to Earth that lowered my empathy levels that's to blame! Surely, you can understand."
+1, great job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel as though more explanation as to how the committee fooled the rest of the Foundation into thinking they were aliens. So little explanation is put here it makes it sound like the Foundation sound like they're incredibly gullible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great job! I just noticed this and I am proud to have helped you out. I really enjoy this article and love all the super off-the-wall ideas you come up with. Still love the overall concept here, the future viewpoint is a wonderful addition, and the execution is on-point! You seriously have earned all the upvotes you have gotten.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't normally like to respond to comments because I personally want my stuff to stand on its own but at this point it's been a month since I wrote it and this reply is on page 2 of the comments so I feel like it's unlikely anyone will treat this as "word of god" or whatever. This is also effectively a reply to DarkStuff, who said similar things on the previous page.

Anyway, I did think a lot about this particular point - the idea that it lets humanity off too easily for its various atrocities and bloody history - while writing this. I think it's actually a totally valid point but I'd like to put forward another way to look at it that I think could spawn an interesting discussion about compulsion effects in general.

+ Show a bunch of words
- Hide em

So the main two complaints levied against compulsion effects are that (1) they're a narrative cop-out, a shortcut for making characters do terrible things for the sake of narrative and (2) it makes the compelled people abdicate responsibility in a way that is strictly less interesting than if they did the terrible things of their own free will. Hopefully we can agree that the first point, while completely valid, is not really an issue for this particular story. The second one, however, I do take some issue with.

We do, of course, have plenty of "compulsion effects" in the real world; for example, drugs and alcohol. Consider the following scenarios: In the first, a man, possessing full mental faculties, cold-heartedly plots to murder a family and then does so. In the second, a man has too much to drink and plows into that family while driving his car. These two scenarios are horrible for different reasons - the first because of the malicious intent behind it, but the second because of the lack of intent. In the second scenario, had the man been of sound mind, everyone would be alive. I'll leave it to moral relativists to decide which is worse, but I'd definitely argue that just because that second scenario is more mundane doesn't inherently make it less terrible.

Of course, I'm glossing over an important choice the second man had - the choice to not get blackout drunk without at least giving his car keys to someone else. It is exactly this choice that compulsion effects remove, and I'd argue that this is the only way for compulsion effects to be framed that makes them effective horror tools: Not as loss of responsibility, but rather loss of control. They rob us of the only form of recompense available to victims, the knowledge of who to blame, because the perpetrator was also a victim.

Moving from generalities to specifics, my SCP turns humanity into a species of drunk drivers, careening pointlessly and tragically off each other for millennia. It renders nearly all of human history irrelevant - a dumb, stupid series of mistakes we didn't even know we were making. While it's framed optimistically in certain parts of the story, it's a completely nihilistic view of everything we've ever done and posits that we've fundamentally been wasting our time.

I'd like to note here that this is not a worldview I share in reality at all. This is a work of fiction, and as such I tried to think of the worst thing that could be true. This is what I came up with - the idea that free will is a joke and human struggle is idiotic.

If you feel like I didn't execute this in a way that brings all these points across, that's valid criticism, please let me know so I can try to do better the next time. If you still feel the premise is fundamentally flawed - I totally understand that! It's hard to get away from the thought that, in the world of this SCP, every mass-murderer was just a poor misunderstood soul. I tried to address this in a sense via the note at the end, where a human who is finally in full possession of his faculties chooses to be the monster anyway (this is also why I tried to make it ambiguous about if O5-3 is being completely honest or if he is self-servingly holding onto his terrestrial empire).

I realize that's a lot of words as a response to such a short comment but it's something I've been mulling over for a while and I wanted to get it out there. Again, I totally respect your take on this and I don't think it's in any way wrong, just wanted to provide an alternate perspective.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you can only bring yourself to appreciate art you agree with, I frankly pity the amount of great things you're missing out on.

Thinking that one must not bring their personal beliefs into their taste and opinions on media is reductive and assumes that there is not already a metric fuckton of art those people can enjoy. I'm not missing out on anything by not reading Lovecraft, because there's a million other things I could be reading, like Boogiepop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lovecraft's thesises and themes are usually fine, especially in his later more mythos-centric work, and still relevant today (maybe more even in a world where it turns out that Bohr was mostly correct, there are enough nuclear weapons to exterminate us a few times, multiple organized genocides have since occured, most of the universe is now known to be invisible, and we might just be in a false vacuum or ancestor simulation anyway). What's problematic about Lovecraft (especially early Lovecraft, but it never goes away entirely) is the casual racism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I chose Lovecraft for two reasons: his work directly inspired a lot of what's on this site, and he is a prime example of "great storytelling with terrible lessons."

The point I'm trying to get at is that what you call the "thesis" does not equal the work in its total. If you can only bring yourself to appreciate art you agree with, I frankly pity the amount of great things you're missing out on. We're talking about stories, not sermons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
criticizing fiction on the sole basis that you disagree with its message is bad form

a few points:

- i dont know why you chose lovecraft

- a fundamental, inextricable part of almost every single story on the face of the earth is the thesis. all artifices of the work serve to propel the theses of it [sometimes this isn't true but that's wasteful writing, imo]. it's entirely reasonable to critique something based on the theses because they inform the work as a whole.

if you think the thesis is bad, you can call a work bad, and thats good

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm never a fan of "people aren't the problem, [thing] is". Technically the most logical conclusion one can go to with this, but I still don't like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Never a big fan of long note at the end syndrome, but the intriguing concept and the geopolitical situations you depict are fascinating enough to easily secure a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Masterpiece

+1

where is the +10 button?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ok this is beautiful +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One -J away from reading enjoyably

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is my favorite of the entire contest. The implications are just…mind-boggling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Humans are greedy and mean, and I get that it’s fiction, but I can not enjoy something that says otherwise. I’ve simply never bought the “humans are special” mentality.

Humans aren't any worse than the average animal on the planet. Acting like we are all bad by default is equally, if not more, disingenuous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
With all due respect, criticizing fiction on the sole basis that you disagree with its message is bad form in a way that I almost find morally unsettling. For instance, a lot of the works on this site are based on the writings of H.P. Lovecraft, who was an outright terrible person who put "messages" in his stories that are far worse than "humans are actually inherently good." But we still love those stories, not because they say good things (they very clearly don't,) but because they're good as stories.

I hope you reconsider your downvote. You admit the article itself is good, clever, and does what it sets out to do - and I agree. Whether or not is clashes with your own personal opinions should be entirely irrelevant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconded on both counts. A compulsion that's actively affecting all humans worldwide is a neat twist, and it being one that actually needs to be preserved in small, controlled population as it's still vital for the species' continued survival (much as it was probably key to our rise up the food chain) adds a layer of Shamayalanness.

But I didn't get the "GoI gets one over the Foundation" bit until it was pointed out, because in the kayfabe version it was still just some rogue agents within the SCP itself (does that count as a GoI/PoI when it's internal bad actors?), and under the reveal it's just a deliberate act by the Foundation administration proper with the truth simply being classified at a higher level than most of the staff has access to. Are the overseers and other high level directorship (including the EC) considered GoIs now, because of the great number of things they know and do that the rest of the organisation lack the clearance to be privy to?

There's probably other cliches that could be applied even so if only having one is considered not enough, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Or more simply: "SCP-4266 is in fact spread by a known vector".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, but why are we like that? In this case, SCP-4266 is the reason.

IRL, we simply haven't got a sufficiently large, sufficiently generalised (instead, a mere handful of specially-selected professionals) offworld population yet that any valid comparisons can be drawn between the behaviour of the lucky few who get to call themselves astronauts and the many billions who will spend their entire lives bound to the Earth.

The skip is instead written from a far-future (roughly 23rd century) perspective where tens of thousands (at least) of people from all walks of life left Earth for the colonies in the last 21st, where the expected social problems traceable to humans being inherently nasty markedly failed to manifest.

Sure, the terrestrial human population is demonstrably made up of borderline psychopaths. But what if that was because of a low level Cordyceps type pathogen (which, say, young children tend to take a couple of years to become infected with…), rather than solely a product of evolution, or worse still "it just is"?

I mean, the entire site is made up of speculative, fantastic fictions. What makes this particular one harder to stomach than, say, a statue made of reinforced concrete that breaks your neck unless you maintain unbroken line of sight on it? That's also clearly not real (…well, we hope, anyway). It's meant as entertainment, and something that gives you a bit of a spook. The spook here being "something that makes you a little bit crazy, and is an infection that you'd quickly recover from if kept quarantined from it … but it's in the air worldwide… there's no escape from it, you're breathing it in continually and being reinfected, with your immune system opting to ignore it because it was an active evolutionary advantage to allow that killer instinct to incorporate itself"…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, for the image caption: remove "the most specific". It's redundant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Let's start with the most basic thing: SCP-4266 is not spread by an unknown vector.

(Which implies that it is not spread by a vector at ALL)

should be

Let's start with the most basic thing: the vector by which SCP-4266 is spread is not unknown.

Personally, I like this one. Have an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I downvoted within the first paragraph of the description. Because this follows the escapism that a good number of fictions on this site do: that humans aren’t to blame for all the shitty things we do. Shifting the blame always leaves a bad taste in my mouth. It’s nice to think we’re all cool, but we’re not. Humans are greedy and mean, and I get that it’s fiction, but I can not enjoy something that says otherwise. I’ve simply never bought the “humans are special” mentality.

Anyways, your writing is alright (though I think you should get more familiar with commas) and I see how this sprouted as an idea for clichecon. Your use of the trope is clever and for that I commend you. It’s also a good length for the idea it delivers. I just personally can’t support this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When the macho dad comes to protect his kind innocent children.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is outstanding.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of the best I've read. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love this. +1.
Tiny nitpick: "mass exodus" is a redundancy, since exodus already means a mass movement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the best telling of the “earth is the problem, not humans” trope possibly ever. +100

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A really cool take on the "compulsion effect" cliche. I really like the ethical approach of attempting to cause as minimal damage from violence during the migration period, as well as the pragmatic realization that violence is ultimately necessary. +1

Though, I'm not a huge fan of the "Ethics Committee as a GoI" thing. I've held the view that the Foundation is strongest when its two goal-shaping organs, the O5 and EC, work together to secure, contain, and protect. I would even argue that some of the Foundation's greatest containment failures come from an inability to be ethical enough. Generally, I find scips that treat the EC as "annoying intellectuals who get in the way of containment" to not really understand the kind of consequentialist ethics the Foundation uses so well.

That said, I don't think that impacts the story much here, and the rest is so good, so it's an upvote from me.

item.title: Author post
item.content:encoded:
This is an entry for cliche-con.

+Cliches used (spoilers!)
-Hide
  • Compulsion effect that makes you do bad things/kills you.
  • (GoI/PoI here) gets one over the Foundation, making them look like fools in the process.

This is a Category 3 entry.

Thanks to the following people who provided critique:
Forums: Ensophos, Reverend Fox, Shanor, Zyn
Chat: thewalkindude, PopcornK, DrKetos, rounderhouse

Image is from here and is public domain: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:2016_Top_Images_from_NASA_Goddard_(31786791410).jpg
